clinical_history: The following ECG from a critically-ill middle-aged woman with shock indicates which diagnosis?
diagnosis: d) The ECG shows sinus tachycardia, borderline low QRS voltage, and marked ST segment elevations in the inferior and lateral leads, along with ST segment depressions in anterior leads V1-V4. The ST elevations (current of injury pattern) indicate transmural ischemia of the infero-lateral wall. The ST depressions are most consistent in this context with reciprocal change from the ST elevations generated by the acute posterior and lateral ischemia. (Concomitant anterior subendocardial ischemia cannot be excluded as a cause of the ST depressions). In contrast, acute pericarditis causes diffuse ST segment elevation (e.g., leads I, II, III, aVL, aVF, and the precordial leads). Reciprocal ST depressions of the type seen here (V1-V4), however, are never a feature of pericarditis alone. Acute pulmonary embolism may produce changes simulating acute MI, including new Q waves in leads III and aVF with ST-T changes (S1Q3T3 pattern) or T wave inversions in the right to mid-chest leads. However, the findings seen here are not consistent with acute cor pulmonale/right ventricular overload due to pulmonary embolism. Hyperkalemia also does not produce ST elevations in this distribution. (ST elevations may occur in leads V1-V2 or V3 with marked hyperkalemia).  Severe systemic hypothermia may be associated with a characteristic bulging of the early part of the ST segment (Osborn waves or J waves), but not with the classic localized current of injury pattern with reciprocal ST changes present in this ECG.Cardiac catheterization revealed acute occlusion of a dominant left circumflex coronary artery (along with occlusion of a smaller right coronary artery.) Echocardiography confirmed extensive wall motion abnormalities of the inferior, posterior and lateral segments of the left ventricle. The anterior wall was hyperkinetic.
